_meta.purpose: AGRIVEDA Smart Crop Advisory - VEGETABLES ONLY, supplements agriveda_crop_calendar_india.json
_meta.why_a_separate_file: The main crop-calendar file only used a generic 'Vegetables' placeholder for most zones/states. Vegetables behave differently from grain/pulse/oilseed crops - seasonality is looser, regional specialisation is sharper, and a handful of states dominate specific vegetables nationally. That deserved its own file.
_meta.seasonality_caveat: Kharif/Rabi/Zaid is a simplification for vegetables. Tomato, brinjal, chilli, and okra are often grown in staggered batches almost year-round wherever irrigation allows, with different varieties suited to each window - the season listed below is the PRIMARY/heaviest one, not the only one.
_meta.hill_state_note: Jammu & Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh, and hill Uttarakhand grow 'off-season' vegetables in their summer (which is plains' too-hot Zaid season) - tomato, capsicum, cabbage, peas grown there fill the gap when plains supply is thin.

national_vegetable_leaders.potato_aloo: Uttar Pradesh #1 (~30-35% of national output; Agra-Mainpuri-Firozabad-Kannauj belt) > West Bengal #2 > Bihar > Gujarat > Punjab
national_vegetable_leaders.onion_pyaz: Maharashtra #1 (Nashik belt, over one-third of national output) > Madhya Pradesh > Gujarat > Karnataka - these 4 states together make ~two-thirds of India's onion
national_vegetable_leaders.tomato_tamatar: Madhya Pradesh leads national output, with Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, and Gujarat also major producers
national_vegetable_leaders.brinjal_baingan: West Bengal #1 (~24-28% of national output, Nadia/24-Parganas/Hooghly belt) > Odisha #2 > Gujarat #3
national_vegetable_leaders.tapioca_kand: Tamil Nadu + Kerala together = ~94% of national output
national_vegetable_leaders.ginger_adrak: Madhya Pradesh, Karnataka, Gujarat lead in plains; Meghalaya and Sikkim lead in the hills

uttar_pradesh_zones.note: Closest UP zone to the Delhi-NCR mandi belt - highest vegetable diversity and cropping intensity in the state
Agra-Mainpuri-Firozabad is one of India's single biggest potato pockets, with good cold-storage access - the single strongest vegetable bet in this zone
Water scarcity keeps overall vegetable area small; garlic/onion tolerate the dry conditions far better than a water-hungry crop like potato
Varanasi belt also well known for paan (betel leaf) alongside its vegetables

india_states.note: Summer here = plains' off-season - a niche high-value vegetable window
India's best-known off-season vegetable hub - supplies plains mandis when local plains supply is thin
See 'uttar_pradesh_zones' above. UP is India's #1 overall vegetable-producing state (~15% of national output) and #1 in potato specifically.
Strong, growing onion and brinjal producer
National leader in tomato and garlic; among the top 4 onion producers
India's largest onion producer (Nashik belt) - uniquely grows onion across all three seasons for near year-round supply
Major onion, tomato, and chilli producer
Guntur is India's chilli trading hub; 2nd-largest tomato producer
Tamil Nadu + Kerala grow ~94% of India's tapioca
Perennial/tuber crops dominate over the classic Kharif-Rabi-Zaid vegetable cycle
National #1 in brinjal (~24-28%), cabbage (~24-34%), cauliflower (~22-29%) and okra (~14-19%); #2 in potato
Rainfed - vegetable area much smaller than grain area
Skilled short-cycle vegetable farmers - multiple harvests per year on the same plot
Tea-dominant state; vegetable area is comparatively small

india_states.state: North-Eastern Hill States (Meghalaya, Manipur, Mizoram, Nagaland, Tripura, Sikkim, Arunachal Pradesh)
india_states.note: Meghalaya and Sikkim are leading ginger belts

india_states.state: Delhi / Chandigarh / Puducherry / Andaman & Nicobar / Lakshadweep
india_states.note: Small, peri-urban or highly localised vegetable production - not broken out in detail here
